coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes and their photoelectrochemical activities for water splitting
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Sang, Lixia
Lin, Jia
Zhao, Yangbo
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots with various functional groups can be employed as the potential sensitizer. In this study, carbon dots are obtained by electrochemical ablation of graphite rods in alkaline electrolyte. The better preparation condition is the applied potential of 40 V and the ablation time of 5 h. TiO2 nanotube arrays and TiO2 nanoparticles photoelectrodes are sensitized by the as-prepared carbon dots through using impregnation method. Carbon dots/TiO2 nanotube arrays electrodes exhibit greater photoelectrochemical hydrogen production activities than carbon dots/TiO2 nanoparticles electrodes. It is because more carbon dots can be well combined with TiO2 nanotube arrays. Based on the IPCE values in visible light region, the role of carbon dots on TiO2 nanotube arrays electrode depends on the up-converted PL behaviors from their surface states and the alkaline electrolyte. The results provide insight into carbon dots that serve as sensitizer of TiO2 photoelectrode in water splitting system of alkaline solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
TiO2
Sensitization
Photoelectrochemical properties
Water splitting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of carbon dots sensitized TiO2 electrodes
Tuning of carbon dots solution by changing time and potential
The constituent of carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes based on XRD, SEM and EDS
The photoelectrochemical properties of carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes
The photoelectrochemical activity from UVDRS, IPCE and Tafel slope
The charge–transfer property from MS and EIS

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2017 Hydrogen Energy Publications LLC. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: LSV plots of (a) TiO2 NP electrode and TiO2 NP electrodes sensitized. Carbon dots solutions were prepared at applied potential of 20 V for different time of (b) 4 h, (c) 5 h and (d) 6 h, respectively.

LSV plots of (a) TiO2 NP electrodes and carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrodes. Carbon dots solutions were prepared at applied potential of (b) 20 V, (c) 30 V and (d) 40 V, respectively, while the reaction time was set as 5 h.

(a) TEM image of the carbon dots prepared at 40 V for 5 h in alkaline solution. Inset of (a) is size distribution histogram estimated from TEM measurements of carbon dots. (b) Up-converted PL spectra of the as-prepared carbon dots with varying excitation wavelengths (λ = 360, 380, 400, 420, 440 and 460 nm).
XRD patterns of (a) carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode and (b) carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrode. Carbon dots were prepared at the applied potential of 40 V for 5 h.

SEM images of TiO2 NT electrode (a) without and (b) with carbon dots. TEM images of (c) TiO2 NP electrodes with carbon dots and (d) SAED images showing the lattice spacing of TiO2 nanoparticles and carbon dots. EDS images of (e) TiO2 NT electrodes with carbon dots, (f) TiO2 NP electrodes with carbon dots. Carbon dots were prepared at 40 V for 5 h in alkaline solution.
I–t plots of (a, c) TiO2 electrodes and (b, d) TiO2 electrodes which were sensitized by carbon dots which were prepared at the applied potential of 40 V for 5 h. Irradiation: 100 mW/cm2, AM 1.5G.

UV–Vis DRS of (A) TiO2 NP electrodes (a) and carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrodes (b), and (B) TiO2 NT electrode (a) and carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode (b). Carbon dots were prepared at the applied potential of 40 V for 5 h.
IPCE plots of (a, c) TiO2 electrodes and (b, d) TiO2 electrodes sensitized by carbon dots. Carbon dots were prepared at the applied potential of 40 V for 5 h.

Polarization plots of (a) TiO2 NT electrode and (b) TiO2 NT electrode sensitized by carbon dots in 0.1 M KOH under irradiation (100 mW/cm2, AM 1.5G). Carbon dots were prepared at the applied potential of 40 V for 5 h.

Mott–Schottky plots of (A) TiO2 NP electrodes (a) and carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrodes (b), and (B) TiO2 NT electrode (a) and carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode (b). Carbon dots were prepared at the applied potential of 40 V for 5 h.

Nyquist plots of (a) TiO2 NT electrode and (b) carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode in 0.1 M KOH. Carbon dots were prepared at the applied potential of 40 V for 5 h. Irradiation: 100 mW/cm2, AM 1.5G. The inset shows the equivalent circuit (R S: the solution resistance, R ct: the solution resistance and CPE: the capacitance of the double-layer.).
Results from EDS analysis of TiO2 electrodes and carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes a .

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Carbon dots were prepared at the applied potential of 40 V for 5 h. TiO2 electrodes were sensitized by carbon dots for 18 h; the geometrical area of photoelectrode was 1 cm2.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: All measurements of Au were taken outside because of high background levels of Au.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes and their photoelectrochemical activities for water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Enhanced Heat Transfer and Energy Conservation of Ministry of Education, Key Laboratory of Heat Transfer and Energy Conservation Beijing Municipality, Department of Energy Science and Engineering, College of Environmental and Energy Engineering, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Enhanced Heat Transfer and Energy Conservation of Ministry of Education
Key Laboratory of Heat Transfer and Energy Conservation Beijing Municipality
Department of Energy Science and Engineering
College of Environmental and Energy Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots with various functional groups can be employed as the potential sensitizer. In this study, carbon dots are obtained by electrochemical ablation of graphite rods in alkaline electrolyte. The better preparation condition is the applied potential of 40 V and the ablation time of 5 h. TiO2 nanotube arrays and TiO2 nanoparticles photoelectrodes are sensitized by the as-prepared carbon dots through using impregnation method. Carbon dots/TiO2 nanotube arrays electrodes exhibit greater photoelectrochemical hydrogen production activities than carbon dots/TiO2 nanoparticles electrodes. It is because more carbon dots can be well combined with TiO2 nanotube arrays. Based on the IPCE values in visible light region, the role of carbon dots on TiO2 nanotube arrays electrode depends on the up-converted PL behaviors from their surface states and the alkaline electrolyte. The results provide insight into carbon dots that serve as sensitizer of TiO2 photoelectrode in water splitting system of alkaline solution.

ce:section.ce:para: Hydrogen has received considerable attention as a next generation energy carrier [1]. Since Fujishima and Honda [2] reported that hydrogen can be evolved from the photoelectrochemical splitting of water on TiO2 in 1972, the extensive researches have been conducted to solar-hydrogen technology on semiconductor materials [3–5]. TiO2 has become one of the most attractive semiconductor materials for the solar-hydrogen conversion, because it is abundant, cheap and chemically stable [6–8]. However, due to the large band gap (3.2 eV), TiO2 is only active in the ultraviolet (UV) region. That is to say, it can only use less than 5% of the total energy of the solar spectrum. Moreover, the rapid recombination of the photogenerated electron–hole pairs can greatly reduce the energy conversion efficiency of TiO2. Therefore, many efforts including doping by the impurities [9,10] and sensitization with the organic dyes or the narrow band gap semiconductors such as CdS [11], CdSe [12], and PbS [13] have been taken to extend the optical absorption into visible-light region and promote the separation of the photogenerated electron–hole pairs. Although the organic dyes can drastically increase the photocurrent density, dye molecules are easily desorbed from TiO2 surface, resulting in significant losses in quantum efficiency of TiO2 electrodes [14]. When using CdS and PbS quantum dots as sensitizers, the toxic elements of Cd and Pb are harmful to the environment. It is crucial to develop an environmental friendly sensitizer of TiO2.

Carbon dots (C dots, CDs) are the small carbon particles with size below 10 nm. Since carbon dots were discovered unexpectedly by researchers purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs) fabricated by arc-discharge methods in 2004 [15], they have been widely investigated in the areas of bioimaging [16], fluorescent sensing [17], organic dyes degradation [18]. But they are relatively few applied in the photocatalytic water splitting system. Very importantly, Kang et al. [19] demonstrated that the carbon dots-C3N4 has an impressive performance for photocatalytic solar water splitting with the quantum efficiencies of 16% for wavelength λ = 420 ± 20 nm C3N4 and carbon dots are responsible for the first step (photocatalysis for H2 generation) and the second step (chemical catalysis for O2 generation), respectively. Carbon dots also increase the light absorbance and thus the values of the quantum efficiency and the “solar-to-hydrogen” conversion efficiency, which indicates that carbon dots can be a promising sensitizer.

Carbon dots can usually be prepared by laser ablation of carbon [20], oxidation of candle soot with nitric acid [21] and microwave synthesis [22]. Sun et al. [20] prepared carbon dots via laser ablation of a carbon target in the presence of water vapor with argon as carrier gas, followed by refluxing in 3–5 M nitric acid overnight. Zhu et al. [22] dissolved glucose and PEG-200 into the distilled water to form a clear solution, followed by putting into a microwave oven for different time periods. Zhou et al. [23] obtained carbon dots from the multiwalled carbon nanotube by the electrochemical method in 0.1M tetrabutylammonium perchlorate (TBAP). Recently, it is worth noting that the carbon dots with size of 1.2–3.8 nm can be prepared by facile one-step alkali-assisted electrochemical method [18]. Li et al. [18] synthesized carbon dots by using graphite rods as both anode and cathode under including ethanol alkaline solutions with a current intensity in the range of 10–200 mA/cm2. Zhang's group [24] synthesized carbon dots by using graphite rods as both anode and cathode with a static potential of 60 V for seven days under intense magnetic stirring. The solution of carbon dots was filtered and centrifuged to separate off the large particles and then concentrated to 0.1 mg mL−1 by rotary evaporation at 80 °C. Yu et al. [25] synthesized carbon dots by using graphite rods as both anode and cathode under including ethanol alkaline solutions with a current intensity of 20 mA/cm2. The solution of carbon dots was dialyzed using deionized water in a dialysis bag for one day. In summary, this method is easy to operate and has a time-efficient process, whose materials also can be obtained easily. Currently, one major issue is how to prepare the carbon dots quickly and conveniently. Although the numbers of articles relative to this area are abundant, it is lack of optimization of alkali-assisted electrochemical conditions for fabrication of carbon dots. Moreover, the properties of the as-prepared carbon dots depend on the resulting carbon dots solution during electrochemical process. More studies should be oriented to controlling the concentration of carbon dots solution by changing the experimental parameters, such as applied potential and oxidation time.

Carbon dots possess the abundant functional groups on the surface, which is helpful to be sensitizer. Some studies have been done on sensitization of semiconductor such as TiO2 [24], Fe2O3 [26], Ag3PO4 [27], BiOI [28] and ZnO [29] with carbon dots. Therefore, another important issue is how to better combine the carbon dots and the semiconductor to obtain the enhanced photoelectrochemical properties. Yu et al. [30] reported that the carbon dots/TiO2 nanosheet composites possess higher transfer efficiency of photogenerated electrons and separation efficiency of photogenerated electron–hole pairs than carbon dots/P25 composites. It is found that TiO2 nanosheets with 68% of exposed (001) facets, are combined with carbon dots through surface carbon-oxygen groups. Wang et al. [31] proposed the bidentate complexes of a green carbon source (vitamin C, VC) for the preparation of TiO2/carbon dots nanocomposites. The in situ transformed carbon dots from VC can ensure the formation of chemically coupled heterostructures, TiO2/carbon dots, which is favor to electron transfer. The resulting TiO2/carbon dots photocatalyst exhibits a higher H2 evolution rate from photocatalytic reactions up to 9.7 times than that of bare TiO2.

For the enhanced photoelectrochemical properties, it is commonly known that carbon dots not only extend the spectral responsive range of TiO2 to absorb more sunlight but also act as an electron capturer of the photogenerated electrons from TiO2 [32]. Particularly, the surface states of carbon dots may play great role on trapping the photogenerated electrons [33]. The surface states are predominantly composed of different oxygen-containing functional groups (epoxy, carbonyl and carboxyl) and carbon atoms on the edge of the carbon framework. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of carbon dots are derived from the surface state on the carbon dots. Application study on the photoluminescence (PL) properties is regarded as up-converted PL behaviors [34,35]. Li et al. [36] reported the up-converted PL behaviors of carbon dots. When the carbon dots/Cu2O composite photocatalyst is illuminated, carbon dots can absorb near IR light, and then emit shorter wavelength light (390–564 nm), which in turn further excites Cu2O to form electron/hole (e−/h+) pairs. Photocatalytic properties will be improved due to the up-converted PL behaviors. Zhang et al. [27] confirmed carbon dots with excellent up-converted PL behaviors can indeed make the carbon dots/Ag3PO4 and carbon dots/Ag/Ag3PO4 complex photocatalysts effectively utilize the full spectrum of sunlight to greatly enhance the photocatalytic properties.

In the present work, carbon dots were prepared by the electrochemical ablation of graphite rods in alkaline electrolyte, and the better carbon dots solution was obtained by tuning the reaction time and the applied potential. Then carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes were fabricated via impregnation method for the photoelectrochemical splitting of water. To study how to better combine the carbon dots and the semiconductor, different TiO2 electrode morphologies were also prepared. The effects of TiO2 electrode morphologies on the photoelectrochemical performance are investigated in details. We also focus on exploring the origin of IPCE response of carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes in KOH electrolyte under visible light irradiation.

Titanium foils (0.25 mm thick, 99.5% purity) were supplied by Alfa-Aesar Chemical Technology Co. Ltd. Phosphate acid (H3PO4, ≥85% in water), sodium fluoride (NaF, ≥98%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥98%) were purchased from Tianjin Fuchen Chemical Reagents Factory. Potassium hydroxide (KOH, ≥85%), ethanol (AR) and acetone (AR) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Corporation. All solutions were prepared using high-purity deionized water. Titania pastes (with an average particle diameter of ∼20 nm) were purchased from Dyesol. Ltd and FTO (7 Ω cm−1, 5 cm2) from Organic Photovoltaic Tech New Energy Co. Ltd.

Two graphite rods (99.99%, Tianjin Ai Da technology Co. Ltd) were perpendicularly inserted in a 100 ml beaker containing a mixture of deionized water and ethanol, which were used as anode and cathode with a separation of 3.5 cm, respectively. Different values of static potentials were applied between the two electrodes using a direct current (DC) power supply for different reaction time. During the process, the solution quickly changed from colorless to yellow and finally to dark brown. Afterwards, the solution was filtrated to remove impurities and large particles, resulting in carbon dots solution. Li et al. [18] indicated that alkaline environment is the key factor of the electrochemical oxidation process, and OH–group is essential for the formation of carbon dots. Thus, we created alkaline environment for preparing carbon dots solution by adding 0.4 g NaOH.

TiO2 nanoparticle (TiO2 NP) electrode was prepared as follows: FTO substrates were carefully cleaned in acetone, ethanol and deionized water in sequence for 15 min under ultrasonication. Then, the FTO substrate was coated with titania pastes by a doctor-blading method. The coated area of titania pastes is 1 cm2. Finally, the as-prepared samples were firstly annealed in air at 180 °C for 60 min, then heated to 450 °C and lasted for 60 min. The heating rate was set as 1 °C/min.

TiO2 nanotube array (TiO2 NT) electrode was prepared by anodic oxidation method according to our previous report [7]. The anodization of Ti foil was carried out in an aqueous solution of 0.5M H3PO4 and 0.14 M NaF for in ultrasonication irradiation (Kunshan Ultrasonic Instrument Co. Ltd., KQ-100DE). A platinum gauze (20 mm × 30 mm) was counter electrode and 20 V DC voltage was supplied by DC power (Maynuo M8812, 0–2 A, 0–75 V) at room temperature for 1 h. After anodization, samples were washed with deionized water immediately to remove absorbed ions, and annealed at 450 °C for 4 h in air.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots sensitized TiO2 electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The carbon dots were directly grown on the TiO2 electrodes surfaces by impregnation method [31,37]. The experimental process is as follows: the prepared TiO2 NP electrode or TiO2 NT electrode was immersed into carbon dots solution for 18 h, followed by rinsing with deionized water to remove the remaining carbon dots solution from the electrode surface, and finally dried in air at room temperature.

The surface morphologies of TiO2 NT electrodes and carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrodes were characterized with a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM; Hitachi, Japan, S4300). The microstructures of carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrodes were analyzed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM; JEOL Ltd., Japan, JEM-2010). The crystal structures of the samples were determined by using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD; D8 Advance Bruker/AXS). The ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectra of the samples were measured by an ultraviolet–visible spectrometer (UV–Vis DRS; Shimadzu, Japan, UV-2450) and compressed BaSO4 powder as standard. Photo luminescence spectra of carbon dots were monitored with the fluorescence spectrophotometer (PL; Fluorolog-3 Jobin Yvon Inc.)

The photoelectrochemical (PEC) experiments were carried out in a three-electrode system with a platinum gauze (20 mm × 30 mm) counter electrode, a saturated Ag/AgCl reference electrode and the prepared sample working electrode in 1M KOH. A solar simulator (91160, Newport, USA) with an AM 1.5 filter was used as light source, adjusting the light intensity to 100 mW/cm2. All the photoelectrochemical properties data were collected by the electrochemical analyzer (Autolab 302N, Switzerland). Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was recorded from −0.4 V to +0.4 V with a slow scan rate of 0.5 mV s−1 in a solution contained 1 M KOH under irradiation. Tafel plots were recorded to evaluate the kinetic parameters within the potential range from −1 V to 0 V under irradiation. I-t curves measurements were recorded under illumination for three 50 s light-on–off cycles. Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) measurements were carried out at room temperature by applying a sinusoidal perturbation of 5 mV amplitude with the frequency range of 10−1 Hz to 104 Hz at the open circuit potentials (OCP). The measured EIS data were fitted by Autolab EIS fitting software. Mott–Schottky plots of the semiconductor electrodes were done to analyze the flat band potential with the frequency of 3 kHz within the potential range of -1 V to 0 V. Incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurements were performed by using a 150W Xe lamp (69901, Newport, USA) as the light source and a monochromator (Czerny-Turner, Crowntech, USA) with the measured range of 300–1100 nm. The production of hydrogen was online detected by an Agilent 7890B gas-chromatograph (5 A molecular sieve, N2 carrier gas).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tuning of carbon dots solution by changing time and potential
ce:section.ce:para: It is important to obtain better carbon dots solution for preparation of the carbon dots sensitized TiO2 electrode. Carbon dots solutions can be obtained by electrochemical ablation of graphite rods under alkaline condition. When setting the applied potential as 20 V, the time for electrochemical ablation of graphite rods was changed as 4, 5 and 6 h. The TiO2 NP electrodes were then sensitized in the above carbon dots solution. Fig. 1 shows the LSV results of the resulting electrodes under irradiation. From Fig. 1, we can see that the carbon dots (5 h)/TiO2 NP electrode exhibits the greatest photocurrent density, while the photocurrent density carbon dots (6 h)/TiO2 NP electrode is similar to that of TiO2 NP electrode. That is, in the case of carbon dots (6 h)/TiO2 NP electrode, carbon dots can't effectively improve the photoelectrochemical properties. It is because the carbon dots become larger and less uniform on TiO2 NP electrode with increasing reaction time [25]. Moreover, the loading content of carbon dots is also increased accordingly. However, too much carbon dots are disadvantageous to contact with TiO2, which will have an adverse impact on the electron transfer between the two components. Therefore, relative to reaction time of 4 h and 6 h, 5 h may be the better condition for preparation of carbon dots solution by electrochemical ablation of graphite rods.

When the reaction time was set as 5 h, the potential for electrochemical ablation was changed as 20 V, 30 V and 40 V. The applied potential is not more than 40 V to save the external energy. Fig. 2 shows the LSV plots of all the TiO2 NP electrodes sensitized in the above carbon dots solution. As shown in Fig. 2, carbon dots prepared at higher applied potential can more effectively increase the photocurrent density of TiO2 NP electrodes. Moreover, it can be found from Figs. 1 and 2 that the applied potential has more effects on carbon dots solution than the reaction time. The photocurrent density of carbon dots (40 V)/TiO2 NP electrodes is about 0.60 mA/cm2 at 0 V versus Ag/AgCl, which is a six-fold increase over that of the pure TiO2 NP electrode. Its photocurrent density can be up to 8.5 mA/cm2 at 0.4 V versus Ag/AgCl. As a result, the carbon dots solution prepared at 40 V for 5 h in alkaline solution are used to impregnate the TiO2 electrodes as follows.

As for the carbon dots prepared in alkaline solution at 40 V for 5 h, their sizes were analyzed by the TEM image which is shown in Fig. 3 a. From Fig. 3a, it can be seen that the carbon dots are spherical and well dispersed. Based on the size distribution histogram of the as-prepared carbon dots, the mean diameter of carbon dots is estimated to be 4.12 nm ± 0.97 nm. Fig. 3b shows the up-converted PL spectra of the synthesized carbon dots solution by using excitation wavelengths ranging from 360 nm to 460 nm with the interval of 20 nm. Carbon dots exhibits excitation-wavelength-dependent PL properties and the PL intensity of carbon dots decreases gradually upon increasing the excitation wavelengths [38,39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The constituent of carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes based on XRD, SEM and EDS
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 4 shows that XRD patterns of the carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode and the carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrode. From Fig. 4, it can be found that the diffraction peaks of anatase TiO2 besides the conductive substrate of titanium or SnO2. However, we cannot confirm that there exists the carbon component on the electrodes from XRD patterns because the characteristic peak of carbon at 26° is overlapped with TiO2 diffraction peak [31].

Fig. 5 shows the SEM images of TiO2 NT electrode with and without carbon dots and the TEM image of TiO2 NP electrode. From Fig. 5a, it can be found that the highly order TiO2 NT arrays have an average tube diameter of ∼100 nm. Fig. 5b demonstrates the growth of carbon dots on TiO2 NT arrays. A representative TEM image of carbon dots on TiO2 nanoparticles is shown in Fig. 5c. The diameter of TiO2 nanoparticles is in the range of 15–20 nm. Fig. 5d shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image of carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrode. Fig. 5d shows the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) image of carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrode. The lattice spacing of 0.24 nm corresponds to the (004) crystal plane of TiO2 nanoparticle, while the lattice spacing of 0.23 nm originates from the carbon dots [21]. Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) in Fig. 5e indicates that the relative amount of the carbon element is 10.30 at %, which can confirm that the carbon dots were loaded on the TiO2 NT electrode successfully.

Based on the EDS, it can found that the pure TiO2 electrodes only exhibit two typical energy dispersion peaks for Ti and O, while an additional strong peak in the electrodes sensitized by carbon dots corresponds to the deposited carbon on the TiO2 electrodes. The mass percent of three elements of C, Ti and O can be measured by analyzing EDS, and the results are presented in Table 1 .

As shown in Table 1, there are two elements to be detected (Ti, O) in the TiO2 NP and TiO2 NT. The approximate atomic ratio of Ti to O is 1:2, which matches with stoichiometric ratio of TiO2. The amount of C in the carbon dots/TiO2 NT and carbon dots/TiO2 NP is 10.30 and 4.38 mass %, respectively. The reason may be that TiO2 nanotube arrays can absorb more carbon dots due to their one-dimensional tubular structure and the higher specific surface area [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photoelectrochemical properties of carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6 , the average photocurrent density at 0 V vs Ag/AgCl of TiO2 NT electrode, TiO2 NP electrode, carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode and carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrode is about 0.32 mA/cm2, 0.03 mA/cm2, 0.37 mA/cm2 and 0.04 mA/cm2, respectively. At the same time, under the same illumination condition (100 mW/cm2, AM 1.5G), the production of hydrogen from a three-electrode system was online detected at 0 V vs Ag/AgCl. However, hydrogen can't be detected over TiO2 NP electrode and carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrode, while the stable hydrogen production rate of TiO2 NT electrode and carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrode can be about 61.85 μL/h cm2 and 110.45 μL/h cm2 during irradiation of 4 h, respectively. In fact, the hydrogen production rates of the electrodes depend on their photocurrent density [8]. When the photocurrent density is less than 0.3 mA/cm2, the amount of hydrogen production will be too low to be detected by GC. Obviously, the average photocurrent densities of TiO2 NP electrode and carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrode are far below 0.3 mA/cm2. Moreover, the photoelectrochemical activity of TiO2 NP electrode fails to be improved to a large extent by the sensitization of carbon dots. However, the photocurrent density of TiO2 NT electrode can be increased from 0.32 mA/cm2 to 0.37 mA/cm2 by the sensitization of carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The photoelectrochemical activity from UVDRS, IPCE and Tafel slope
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of the TiO2 electrodes with and without carbon dots. It is seen that the loading of carbon dots can enhance light absorption of TiO2 electrodes, specifically for TiO2 NT electrode. The extent of the increase in absorbance for TiO2 NT electrodes is significantly larger than that in TiO2 NP electrodes, which is one reason of the higher photocurrent density of carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode in Fig. 6. From Table 1, we also know that the carbon dots on TiO2 NT electrode are more than that on TiO2 NP electrodes. This suggests that the amount of carbon dots on TiO2 electrodes increases, the absorption of carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes increases. That is, carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrodes can make more use of incident light. The stronger absorption peak in 400 nm for carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode means the better combination between carbon dots and TiO2, which is helpful to charge transfer in photoelectrochemical reaction. Liu [41] suggest that the addition of carbon dots effectively increases the adsorption of TiO2 in the visible light region. Carbon dots exhibit a PL emission peak at 438 nm [42]. Moreover, a slight increase in absorption edge is observed, indicating the carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes are able to utilize a wider range of the solar spectrum for enhanced photocatalytic H2 production. Zou [43] supposed that the visible light absorption band in 400–550 nm is originated form the TiOC bonds.

Incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) is defined as the ratio of the number of electrons by an incident photon at a given wavelength to the number of incident photons [44]. Relative to UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (Fig. 7), IPCE plot can state clearly the degree of light utilization, because it can take into account both the conversion of the absorbed light and the adsorption extent of light. Fig. 8 shows the IPCE plots of TiO2 electrode and carbon dots/TiO2 electrode as a function of the wavelength of the incident light. It is obvious that the IPCE values of TiO2 NP electrode and carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrode are low, and the maximum value of TiO2 NT electrode is only 4% at the wavelength of 350 nm, which are line with their photocurrent densities in Fig. 6. Different from carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrode, the carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode has a higher value of IPCE at 350 nm, which is enhanced about 17% over that of the bare TiO2 NT electrode. The results imply that the carbon dots play more roles on TiO2 NT electrode rather than TiO2 NP electrode in harvesting photons and promoting the generated photocurrent. As shown in Fig. 7, the absorption values of TiO2 NT electrode is significantly larger than that of TiO2 NP electrodes, which may be due to a larger amount of carbon dots and better combination between carbon dots and TiO2 NT.

In addition, although the absorbance of carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode decrease with the increase of wavelength, the IPCE values in Fig. 8 haven't reduced at the range of 500 nm to 750 nm but have a slightly raising. The UV–Vis results in Fig. 7 may be caused by the nature of carbon dots. The IPCE results in Fig. 8 may be resulted from surface states of carbon dots and electrolyte. Zhang et al. [24] also obtained the similar IPCE results for the carbon dots/TiO2 NT photoelectrode in 0.25M Na2S/0.35M Na2SO3 electrolyte. Under irradiation, carbon dots absorb visible light, which emit shorter wavelength light and then in turn excites TiO2 to form electron–hole pairs as a result of up-converted PL behaviors. Owing to the electric field formed on the interface between carbon dots and TiO2, more photogenerated electrons are pulled toward the conduction band of TiO2. However, the action of surface states of carbon dots is still open to debate. Zhang et al. [24] thought that the positive IPCE value from 500 nm to 750 nm is due to the Na2S and Na2SO3 by comparing the IPCE plots of carbon dots/TiO2 NT photoanode measured in 1 M Na2SO4 and 0.25 M Na2S/0.35 M Na2SO3 aqueous solutions. Na2S and Na2SO3 as sacrificial reagents can consume the photogenerated holes to the decrease recombination of electron–hole pairs and promote photocatalytic hydrogen generation. However, we measured the IPCE plots of carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes in 1 M KOH aqueous solutions and got the similar results. Our data provide evidence that the better IPCE activities in visible light of carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrodes can also be obtained in the alkaline electrolyte. For TiO2 NP electrodes, the amount of carbon dots is too low to affect IPCE response under visible light irradiation.

To acquire a deep understanding of the impact of the surface states of carbon dots on the photoelectrochemical reaction on the electrode surface, we measured the polarization plots of TiO2 NT electrode and TiO2 NT electrode modified with carbon dots in alkaline electrolytes (0.1 M KOH) under irradiation, the results are shown in Fig. 9 . The linear portions of polarization plots can be fitted by Tafel equation (h = b log j + a, where j is the current density and b is Tafel slope), yielding Tafel slopes of 56 and 45 mV/dec for TiO2 NT and carbon dots/TiO2 NT, respectively. Evidently, the carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode exhibits a smaller Tafel slope than the TiO2 NT electrode, indicating the larger current density on the carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode surface. Moreover, a higher current density is noted in conjunction with carbon dots sensitize TiO2 NT electrode, which suggests a better photoelectrochemical performance of the electrodes. Tafel slope is an inherent property of the electrocatalyst materials that is determined by the rate-limiting step of the hydrogen HER [45]. The Tafel slope is related to the surface reaction material. The smaller Tafel slope, the better electrode activity. Carbon dots sensitized TiO2 electrodes make Tafel slopes change, leading to different reaction activities of surface material and become finally different reaction rates. The results may be due to surface states of carbon dots. This conclusion is consistent with IPCE and I-t analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The charge–transfer property from MS and EIS
ce:section.ce:para: The flat band potential is an important interface charge–transfer property [8]. More positive flat band potential can make the band bend steeper, which means larger potential energy barrier. The value of flat band potential is approximately equal to the intersection between the tangent of Mott–Schottky curve and the potential axis. Fig. 10 presents Mott–Schottky plots of TiO2 electrodes and carbon dots sensitized TiO2 electrodes. The flat band potential of carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrode (−0.88 V) is similar to that of and TiO2 NP electrode (−0.86 V), and the flat band potential of carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode (−0.56 V) is more negative than that of TiO2 NT electrode (−0.49 V). Obviously, the flat band potential of TiO2 NP electrode has the more negative value than that of TiO2 NT electrode. However, TiO2 NT electrode has better photoelectrochemical performance. It means that the flat band potential has fewer roles on the photoelectrochemical reaction over photoelectrode. Moreover, the carbon dots have fewer effects on the flat band potential of TiO2 electrode, and the extent of effects depends on the interaction between carbon dots and TiO2 substrate. Maybe, the improved photoelectrochemical properties shown in Fig. 6. Largely ascribe to the carbon dots in itself and the enhanced light response as shown in Fig. 7.

Electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) is a powerful tool for studying charge transfer and recombination processes of photoelectrochemical water splitting at electrode/electrolyte interfaces. Fig. 11 is the Nyquist plots of TiO2 NT electrode and carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrodes under illumination. As shown in Fig. 11, carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode has two impedance radii. An impedance radius is observed in the low frequency region, which corresponds to the Nernstian diffusion process. Another impedance radius is found at middle-frequency, which is related to the charge-transfer process, the semicircle radius reflects charger-transfer resistance. We can observe that the radius of the semicircle for carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode is much smaller than that for TiO2 NT electrode under irradiation (AM1.5, 100 mW/cm2). It means that the sensitization with carbon dots can significantly enhance the electron diffusion and transport processes.

The equivalent circuit in Fig. 11, which was obtained by using Autolab EIS fitting software, fits well with our experimental results. The equivalent circuit gives specific results, which R S represents the solution resistance, R ct represents the solution resistance and CPE represents the capacitance of the double-layer. The impendence of the CPE is expressed as Eq. (1) [46].
(1)
Z
CPE
=
Y o
− 1
( j ω )
− n
where Z CPE is the impendence of the CPE, Y o is the magnitude of the CPE, ω is the frequency and n is the diffusion coefficient.

According to the fitting data from the equivalent circuit, the value of R s is 114 Ω/cm2, the value of R ct is 1.15 TΩ/cm2 for the TiO2 NT electrode. The R ct values is 7.69 kΩ/cm2 for the TiO2 NT electrode sensitized by carbon dots, which demonstrates that the carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode exhibits the greater separation efficiency of photogenerated electron–hole pairs and the faster charge transfer than the pure TiO2 NT electrodes. The results also proved that the carbon dots can be well combined with TiO2 NT electrode, which is contributed to their better photoelectrochemical activity.

In conclusion, we obtained carbon dots/TiO2 electrodes through a facile impregnation method. Based on Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV), it is found that the optimal electrochemical ablation conditions of graphite rods in alkaline electrolyte for carbon dots are confirmed as the applied potential of 40 V and the ablation time of 5 h. By analyzing the UV–vis DRS, MS plots and EIS, the as-synthesized carbon dots sensitized TiO2 electrodes can greatly improve the visible-light absorption and reduce the barrier of charge transfer compared with pure TiO2 electrodes. The average photocurrent density at 0 V vs Ag/AgCl of TiO2 NT electrode, TiO2 NP electrode, carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode and carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrode is approximately 0.32 mA/cm2, 0.03 mA/cm2, 0.37 mA/cm2 and 0.04 mA/cm2, respectively. Carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode shows better photoelectrochemical activities than carbon dots/TiO2 NP electrode, which is attributed to combination of more carbon dots and TiO2 NT. The average hydrogen production rate of carbon dots/TiO2 NT electrode can reach up to 110.45 μL/h·cm2 after 4 h of irradiation. According to IPCE and Tafel plots, we reveal that the role of carbon dots on TiO2 NT electrode depends on the up-converted PL behaviors from their surface states and the alkaline electrolyte.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51376013) for their financial support.
